nativeRange.habitat: Found in slow-moving rivers and streams across West and Central Africa, typically in murky, shaded water over dark, muddy substrates with dense vegetation.

decorPreferences: soft sand substrate (critical for schnauzenorgan probing)
dense low-light plants (Anubias, Java fern, Vallisneria)

diet.notes: Primarily accepts live and frozen meaty foods — blackworms, bloodworms, tubifex, small earthworms, and daphnia are preferred. Many specimens initially refuse dried/prepared foods and need targeted feeding with live food before transitioning to frozen. Some individuals can be trained to accept sinking pellets over time, but this is not guaranteed.

avoidWith: other Elephant Nose Fish (unless in very large tanks with extensive cover — minimum group of 5+)
small or shy fish that cannot compete
invertebrates and small shrimp (will be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: By OpenCage; 2192x2189. Sharp full lateral profile of a single specimen showing the trunk-like schnauzenorgan chin extension, elongated dark body, forked caudal fin and pale lateral markings against a natural driftwood/plant backdrop.
By Bjoertvedt; 2273x2433. Sharp group shot of several individuals probing the substrate with their chin extensions; shows the forked tails and pale crescent markings near the tail on multiple fish.

By GnathoMichel; 3072x2048. High-res view that most distinctly shows the diagnostic pale crescent/bar markings between the dorsal and anal fins; composition is slightly busy (flowerpot decor) so kept as a supporting image.

summary: The Elephant Nose Fish (Gnathonemus petersii) is a West and Central African species notable for an unusually large brain relative to its body size and a sensitive chin extension used to probe the substrate for prey. It is best kept singly in a dimly lit, well-planted tank of at least 200 litres with soft sand, and tends to refuse prepared foods.
careNotes:
The Elephant Nose Fish is one of the most cognitively complex freshwater fish available in the hobby, with a brain-to-body mass ratio rivaling that of some primates. It generates a weak electrical organ discharge (EOD) used for navigation, object detection, conspecific communication, and sex recognition — this makes it particularly sensitive to electromagnetic interference from external equipment.

Soft sand substrate is essential: the proboscis-like chin extension (schnauzenorgan) is used to probe sediment for prey, and abrasive substrates can damage this delicate organ. A minimum sand depth of around 5 cm is generally advised.

This species is strictly nocturnal and tends to hide during the day; providing ample caves, driftwood, and low-light conditions will significantly reduce stress. Bright lighting without cover has been known to cause chronic stress and may lead to premature decline.

Feeding can be challenging: newly acquired specimens often refuse prepared and frozen foods, requiring live blackworms or bloodworms to trigger a feeding response. Over weeks to months, some individuals can be transitioned to frozen foods with patient conditioning; a minority never accept non-live prey.

Conspecific aggression is notable — keeping two individuals in anything short of a very large, heavily decorated tank typically results in conflict. If a group must be kept, a minimum of five individuals in a 400 L+ setup with abundant structure is generally considered necessary to distribute aggression.

Highly sensitive to copper-based medications and salt additions — copper treatments should never be used in tanks housing this species. It is also considered an indicator species for water quality and has been known to be among the first to show signs of deteriorating conditions.

Has been reported to recognize individual keepers and show distinct behavioral responses to familiar versus unfamiliar people — a trait documented in scientific research on mormyrid cognition.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Gnathonemus petersii has not been reliably documented in aquarium conditions and is generally considered not achievable by hobbyists. Sexual dimorphism is subtle and not well established for captive identification. No specialized breeding protocols are currently known.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (50 gal / 189 L vs SeriouslyFish 255 L — used 200 L as cautious midpoint), adult size, temperature, pH, hardness, lifespan (6–9 years), diet, and temperament. Minor pH disagreement (6.5–7.2 vs 6.0–7.5); used the broader SeriouslyFish range supported by FishBase.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Order: Osteoglossiformes, Family: Mormyridae), temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), and native range. FishBase maximum size of 35 cm SL is wild-record; captive specimens typically reach 22–25 cm per other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Not documented in captivity. No reliable breeding protocol exists for hobbyists.
